title: 2 Chronicles 4
subtitle: Temple Furnishings

sections.header: Verses 1–10 — The Bronze Altar, the Sea, the Basins

refs.note: The Kings parallel with more detailed measurements.
The tabernacle’s bronze basin for priestly washing. Solomon multiplies it tenfold.

notes.note: The washing basins teach that purification precedes sacrifice. Nothing unclean may approach the altar. Holiness is the precondition for worship. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The Sea holds bātîm shĕlōshet ʾalāpîm (“3,000 baths”) — Kings says 2,000. The discrepancy may reflect different measurement standards or the distinction between capacity and normal fill level. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: The bronze altar is 20 cubits square and 10 cubits high — four times larger than the tabernacle’s altar. The bronze Sea, 10 cubits in diameter, rests on twelve bronze bulls (three facing each cardinal direction). Ten basins for washing, five on each side. The scale of everything exceeds the tabernacle. The Chronicler presents Solomon’s temple as the tabernacle glorified — same functions, vastly larger scale.

heb.paragraph: Bayit (house) carries a deliberate double meaning in Chronicles: Solomon builds God a house (temple), and God promises David a house (dynasty). The interplay between these two 'houses' generates the theological tension that drives the entire narrative: will the dynasty endure? Will the temple remain? Both depend on covenant faithfulness.

notes.note: The record of royal wickedness serves as a sober warning that privilege and position offer no immunity from the consequences of sin. Each king who 'did evil in the eyes of the LORD' demonstrates that departure from God's Word leads inexorably to moral and national decline. The pattern underscores humanity's desperate need for a perfectly righteous King — fulfilled only in Christ.

The Chronicler's perspective consistently emphasizes God's faithfulness to His covenant promises despite Israel's unfaithfulness. This dual theme — human failure and divine grace — is the heartbeat of all Scripture and finds its ultimate resolution in the cross of Christ, where God's justice and mercy meet perfectly (Rom 3:25-26).

The downward spiral of apostasy recorded here serves as a warning to every generation of believers. Sin is never static — it always progresses (James 1:14-15). The remedy is the same in every age: repentance, return to God's Word, and wholehearted dependence on His grace. 'If we confess our sins, He is faithful and just to forgive us' (1 John 1:9).

refs.note: The parallel inventory. Chronicles compresses but preserves the essential items.
The tabernacle furnishing inventory that the temple vessels replicate at grander scale — continuity of sacred design from portable shrine to permanent house.
The destruction of these same vessels by Nebuchadnezzar — the tragic end of everything Solomon's craftsmen built, reversed only by the return under Cyrus.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The multiplication of tables and lampstands from 1 to 10 each may reflect Second Temple practice or the Chronicler’s understanding of the larger temple’s liturgical needs. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: Huram-Abi completes the bronze work: pots, shovels, bowls. Solomon’s gold furnishings include ten lampstands, ten tables, a hundred gold sprinkling bowls. The altar of gold, the court of the priests, and the great court with bronze doors are completed. The Chronicler provides a compressed but comprehensive inventory. The final note: “The doors of the temple were of gold.” Every threshold between the worshipper and God’s presence is gold — precious, permanent, and gleaming.

heb.paragraph: The miqdāš (sanctuary) is the place where heaven and earth overlap — where God's presence dwells among His people in a mediated, structured way. The Chronicler devotes extraordinary attention to the sanctuary because it represents the centre of Israel's identity: a people organized around the worship of their God.

notes.note: The Chronicler records this history not merely as political narrative but as theological instruction. Every event demonstrates God's sovereign governance of His people and His unwavering faithfulness to the Davidic covenant. The believing reader is meant to see in these events the pattern of God's dealings with His church in every age.

The Chronicler wrote for a post-exilic community needing to understand their identity and hope. His message remains vital: God's promises to David are irrevocable, worship is the center of covenant life, and faithfulness — not circumstances — determines blessing. Believers today can take the same comfort: our God is faithful even when we are not (2 Tim 2:13).

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVHe made ten gold lampstands according to the specifications for them and placed them in the temple, five on the south side and five on the north.ESVAnd he made ten golden lampstands as prescribed, and set them in the temple, five on the south side and five on the north.NIVThe Sea stood on twelve bulls, three facing north, three facing west, three facing south and three facing east. The Sea rested on top of them, and their hindquarters were toward the center.ESVIt stood on twelve oxen, three facing north, three facing west, three facing south, and three facing east. The sea was set on them, and all their rear parts were inward.

thread.text: The Kings parallel with more detailed measurements.
The tabernacle’s bronze basin for priestly washing. Solomon multiplies it tenfold.
The parallel inventory. Chronicles compresses but preserves the essential items.

coaching.tip: The bronze Sea holds approximately 17,500 gallons and rests on twelve bulls. The scale is deliberately overwhelming — worship space should dwarf the worshiper. The furnishings communicate proportion: humanity is small; God is vast; the gap between them is the space the temple fills.

prayer_prompt: Temple furnishings were crafted with care and abundance. Consider asking God about the quality of what you offer Him — whether it reflects the best or the convenient.
